title: Dog Swallowed Alkaline Battery: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a battery, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the risks of corrosive burns and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swallowing a battery is a life-threatening medical emergency that causes severe internal chemical burns; you must transport your dog to an emergency veterinarian immediately.

What happens to my dog's body if they swallow a battery?

Alkaline batteries contain corrosive chemicals such as potassium hydroxide or sodium hydroxide. When a dog bites or swallows a battery, the casing can leak, leading to liquefactive necrosis. This process causes deep, painful chemical burns that can dissolve tissues in the mouth, esophagus, and gastrointestinal tract.

Is swallowing a battery a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Transport your dog to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately.
  • Internal damage can occur within minutes, and delayed treatment can lead to life-threatening complications such as esophageal perforation or heavy metal poisoning.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear before seeking professional help.

How does taking a photo of the battery help the veterinarian during triage?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a photo of the package, the device the battery came from, or a matching battery of the same brand.
  • Providing this image helps your veterinarian identify the exact size, chemical composition, and potential for the battery to be a button or disc type.
  • Identifying button batteries is critical as they carry an additional risk of electrical burns.
  • Knowing the dimensions helps the vet decide if the battery is likely to pass or if surgical removal is necessary.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of alkaline batteries by dogs poses a risk of gastrointestinal tract corrosive injury and foreign body obstruction. The alkaline gel within batteries causes liquefactive necrosis of tissues upon contact, potentially resulting in deep burns. Lithium disk or button batteries can lodge in the esophagus, generating a current against the esophageal walls, leading to circular ulcers with the potential for perforation. Radiography is useful to determine the location and passage of the battery; however, some disk batteries are not readily visible. Batteries that have passed through the pylorus will likely pass without issue with dietary management and judicious use of cathartics, while those remaining in the stomach after 48 hours, or those that have been punctured, may require surgical or endoscopic removal to prevent gastric or intestinal ulceration. If the battery casing is suspected to be punctured, emesis should not be induced due to the risk of further corrosive injury. In cases of disk battery ingestion, administering 20 mL boluses of tap water every 15 minutes can help decrease the severity and delay current-induced esophageal ulceration.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3003)

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting if your dog swallowed a battery. If the battery is leaking, the corrosive liquid will cause severe chemical burns to the esophagus as it is brought back up.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of battery ingestion?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include excessive drooling, oral sores, black or tarry stools, abdominal pain, vomiting, and a refusal to eat. However, internal damage can occur before any outward signs are visible.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog milk or water?
faqs.answer: Do not give any food or liquids until you have spoken with a veterinarian. They will provide specific instructions based on how long ago the ingestion occurred.
